title: Ultrabroadband and band-selective thermal meta-emitters by machine learning

data_scope.training_data_regime: intentionally sparse — exact simulation count not stated publicly
data_scope.materials_database: comprehensive multi-material library; exact count not disclosed in public metadata

core_conclusion: A machine learning paradigm achieves multiparameter inverse design of thermal meta-emitters spanning ultrabroadband and band-selective spectral regimes. The three-plane modelling method extends design into full 3D structural space from sparse data. Seven fabricated proof-of-concept devices surpass state-of-the-art optical and radiative cooling performance, providing a generalizable framework for nanophotonic emitter design.
why_it_matters: Breaks the trial-and-error bottleneck in spectral engineering; first general 3D metastructure inverse design approach; sparse-data efficiency reduces simulation cost; seven experimental devices validate ML-generated designs; direct relevance to passive cooling for building energy savings.
why_nature: Paradigm-shift claim that resolves a long-standing bottleneck; cross-disciplinary span (ML, photonics, materials, energy); experimental validation at scale (seven devices); open code and data on Zenodo consistent with Nature open-science expectations.
added_value_to_my_research: Differentiable 3D descriptor analogy -> gradient-interpretable spatial models beyond post-hoc SHAP/LIME
Intermediate physical state latent variable -> physics-informed latent spatial field in graph learning pipeline
Building-envelope TME + city-scale simulation -> spatial optimization layer linking material gains to urban heat exposure and public health
Structured geometric descriptor for sparse-data generalization -> spatial topology as inductive bias for small-N GeoAI tasks
Foundation model pre-training on photonic corpus -> validates fine-tuning geospatial FMs paradigm for spatial health tasks
